cataloging_subject.label: Hali Meiðhad
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous early-thirteenth-century Middle English virginity tract commonly called Hali Meiðhad. The work is distinct from its two surviving manuscript witnesses, Furnivall's 1922 parallel-text edition, U-M's two-witness digital encoding, and this repository's XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Hali Meiðhad
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Early thirteenth century; Bodley 34 witness copied in the first quarter of the thirteenth century
catalog_summary.region: West Midlands / Welsh Marches, probably associated with the Herefordshire border area

notes: This is the standard modern scholarly form. The source edition and U-M item use the spelling Hali Meidenhad; the 2016 METS edition uses Hali Meithhad.

titles.value: Hali Meidenhad: An Alliterative Homily of the Thirteenth Century
notes: This is the title of Furnivall's 1922 edition and U-M's digital item, not necessarily the medieval work's original title.

assertion.notes: The 1922/U-M transcription reports this as A. F. Parker's reading of a very faint red heading at the foot of Bodley 34 fol. 52; letter forms and spacing vary in modern transcriptions.

notes: The author's gender and institutional identity are not securely established; avoid converting rhetorical perspective into biographical fact.

scope.description: The 1922 parallel-text edition used by U-M
The 1866 EETS edition from Cotton Titus D XVIII, revised and expanded in the 1922 source edition

notes: Cockayne's edition directly represents the Cotton witness only; Furnivall's 1922 revision prints the Bodley and Cotton texts in parallel.

assertion.notes: The normalized range is a cataloging approximation. The surviving witnesses establish early-thirteenth-century circulation but do not provide an exact composition year.

date_statements.display: First quarter of the thirteenth century, approximately 1225

places.label: Herefordshire border area
notes: A linguistic and cultural localization associated with the AB Group, not a securely identified town or institution of composition.

assertion.notes: The METS introduction localizes the Katherine/AB Group to an area of Scandinavian settlement on the Welsh marches; this remains broader than a precise composition site.
Proximity to Hereford Cathedral and Wigmore Abbey is contextual evidence, not proof that either institution authored or commissioned the work.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The early Middle English witnesses preserve closely related but distinct forms; the U-M XML keeps them in separate divisions.

scope.description: Biblical and patristic quotations and phrases embedded in the Middle English tract

form_statements.description: Rhythmical, densely alliterative prose often described as poetic prose; the manuscripts lay the English texts out as prose, and the modern METS editors present them as prose rather than verse.

assertion.notes: Furnivall's subtitle calls the work an 'Alliterative Homily,' but alliteration does not by itself make the work verse.
The manuscript calls the work an epistle, and the tract directly addresses a maiden reader.
Homily is Furnivall's edition-level genre label; modern scholarship more specifically calls the work a tract on virginity.

subject_statements.label: Female contemplative and anchoritic spiritual life

assertion.notes: The likely audience is female contemplatives or religious women; a specific institutional audience is not securely proven.

summaries.value: An anonymous early Middle English tract urging a maiden to preserve virginity as spiritual marriage to Christ. It praises the freedom and angelic status of virgin life, contrasts them with the burdens and dangers of earthly marriage, childbirth, and child-rearing, warns against sexual temptation and pride, and closes by recasting virtues as spiritual children.

HMaid.xml does not encode two different works. It presents parallel transcriptions of two witnesses of the same work: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34 and British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII. Witness variation belongs in lineage and locators rather than being counted as two authored items.

extent.description: One prose work represented in two manuscript-witness divisions. The local XML has 2 DIV1, 94 P, 66 PB, 99 MILESTONE, 10 NOTE1, and 72 Q1 elements, with no L or LG elements. The two divisions are witness transcriptions, not two intellectual works or intrinsic chapters.

content_parts.label: Hali Meiðhad
content_parts.summary: The complete virginity tract, preserved in two witnesses and encoded twice in HMaid.xml so the readings can be compared.
notes: Bodley 34's older leaf notation '52b' corresponds to modern recto/verso language as fol. 52v.

related_works.title: The Katherine Group
notes: In Bodley 34, Hali Meiðhad appears with three saints' lives and Sawles Warde in the group now called the Katherine Group.

related_works.title: Ancrene Wisse
notes: The texts share closely related AB language, geographic origin, and concern with female contemplative life; this does not establish common authorship.

related_works.title: Hildebert of Lavardin's letter to the recluse Athalisa
notes: Modern scholarship identifies this Latin letter, patristic commentary, and additional Latin works as sources; Hali Meiðhad is not a simple one-source translation.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/HMaid.xml, FILEDESC and ENCODINGDESC.
evidence.summary: The header identifies Furnivall, U-M's 1997 digital text, the 1922 EETS source edition, its relationship to Cockayne's earlier edition, the two manuscript sources, and omitted source-edition matter.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, HMaid.xml, Bodley 34 witness division.
evidence.locator: DIV1 NODE='HMaid:1', beginning at Bodley 34 leaf 52b
evidence.summary: The first division transcribes the Bodley 34 version, reports the faint red epistle heading, begins with the Latin Audi filia quotation, and preserves embedded Latin and editorial notes.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, HMaid.xml, Cotton Titus D XVIII witness division.
evidence.locator: DIV1 NODE='HMaid:2', beginning at Cotton Titus D XVIII leaf 112 back
evidence.summary: The second division separately transcribes the Cotton version and retains folio/column milestones through fol. 127.

evidence.citation: Element and attribute count of the checked-in HMaid XML, performed 2026-07-11.
evidence.summary: Inspection found 2 DIV1, 94 P, 66 PB, 99 MILESTONE, 10 NOTE1, and 72 Q1 elements, with no L or LG elements; the two DIV1 nodes are labeled as manuscript witnesses.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Hali Meidenhad item information.
evidence.summary: The item identifies Furnivall as editor, Oxford University Press 1922 as publication, and two contents divisions for Bodley 34 and Cotton Titus D XVIII.

evidence.citation: National Library of Ireland, Hali meidenhad: an alliterative homily of the thirteenth century, 1922.
evidence.summary: The catalog identifies Furnivall's edition as a 1922 revision of Cockayne's EETS OS 18, based on Bodley 34 and Cotton Titus D XVIII, with parallel texts on facing pages and a modernized version at the foot of pages.

evidence.citation: Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek / Bayerische Staatsbibliothek, Oswald Cockayne, Hali meidenhad, EETS OS 18, 1866.
evidence.summary: The record identifies Cockayne's 1866 edition from Cotton Titus D XVIII fol. 112c, published by Trübner as EETS OS 18, and links a BSB digitization.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, ff. 14–147.
evidence.locator: Contents, date, languages, and physical characteristics
evidence.summary: The official record places Hali Meiðhad at fols. 112v–127r, dates this manuscript part to the first half of the thirteenth century, records Middle English and Latin, and describes a two-column Gothic manuscript on parchment.

evidence.citation: Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson, eds. and trans., The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), Middle English Texts Series, 2016.
evidence.summary: The scholarly edition identifies Hali Meithhad (Holy Maidenhood) as a thirteenth-century prose virginity tract in Bodley 34 and situates it within the Katherine Group and literature for female recluses.

evidence.citation: Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson, Introduction to The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.locator: Manuscript, Hali Meithhad, audience, region, and textual modes sections
evidence.summary: The introduction dates Bodley 34 to the first quarter of the thirteenth century, localizes the AB Group broadly to the Welsh marches/Herefordshire context, summarizes the work and its Latin sources, discusses its likely female contemplative audience, and explains why the editors treat its rhythmical alliterative style as prose or poetic prose.

open_questions.question: Can Hali Meiðhad's composition be dated more narrowly than the early thirteenth century independently of the surviving witnesses?
next_steps: Review Bella Millett's critical edition and later scholarship on the text's sources and relative chronology.
Keep composition dates separate from the dates of Bodley 34 and Cotton Titus D XVIII.

open_questions.question: Does current linguistic scholarship justify a more precise place of composition than the West Midlands / Welsh Marches association?
next_steps: Consult recent work on AB language and distinguish localization of the archetype, individual witnesses, and composition.
Do not infer authorship by Hereford Cathedral or Wigmore Abbey from geographic proximity.

open_questions.question: Was the work written specifically for anchoresses, for a broader group of female contemplatives, or for religious lay readers?
next_steps: Preserve audience claims as probabilistic unless a source supplies direct evidence.
Review manuscript context and scholarship on the Katherine Group's readership.

open_questions.question: What is the best diplomatic reading of the faint red Bodley 34 heading at fol. 52v?
next_steps: Compare the 1960 Bodley 34 facsimile and, if possible, a new high-resolution color image against Parker's reading in the 1922 edition.
Record alternate readings without silently normalizing the manuscript title.

notes: The U-M title's phrase 'Alliterative Homily' is retained as an edition title but does not override the work-level form classification as prose.
The two manuscript transcriptions in HMaid.xml should remain separately addressable for variant comparison while sharing a single work-level metadata record.
Rights and access for the medieval text, the 1922 edition, U-M encoding, modern editions, and manuscript images are separated in the lineage manifest.
